coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of N-S-GQDs/Bi2S3 microrods with highly photocatalytic performance for Cr(VI) reduction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhenmin
Liu, Xingqiang
Yu, Changlin
Zhou, Wanqin
Li, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Develop new method to deal with heavy metal pollution is one of important topics in environmental protection. In this work, we prepared novel N,S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) modified Bi2S3 microrods (N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3) via a facile one-step hydrothermal method. In hydrothermal process, the produced N,S-GQDs were synchronously doped into Bi2S3 crystals. The introduction of N,S-GQDs largely increases the specific surface area of the catalyst and accelerates the migration of photogenerated carriers. Moreover, N,S-GQDs doping significantly enhanced the light absorption of Bi2S3 crystals in the range of 200–800 nm. The photocatalytic performance of the prepared catalyst was evaluated by photocatalytic reduction removal of aqueous Cr(VI). The results showed that the performance of the composite N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 microrods was significantly improved compared with bare Bi2S3. With optimum molar ratio of N,S-GQDs to Bi2S3, the reduction rate for Cr(VI) to Cr(III) over N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 reaches 70.3% in 80 min. This work provides a simple route for constructing graphene quantum do-s doped metal sulfide semiconductor as a highly efficient photocatalyst for chromium reduction under light irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal method
Bi2S3 microrods
N,S co-doped graphene quantum dots
Cr(VI) reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the pure Bi2S3 and N,S-GQDs doped Bi2S3 microrods.

FT-IR spectra (a) and Raman spectra (b) of prepared Bi2S3 and N,S-GQDs doped Bi2S3 microrods; (c) XPS survey spectrum of 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3; High resolution XPS spectra: (d) Bi 4f, (e) C 2s, (f) S 2s and N 1s.
SEM and TEM images of the prepared samples. (a–c) SEM images of pure Bi2S3; 0.8N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 and 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3; (d–f) HRTEM images of the 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 sample.

(a) The N2 absorption-desorption isotherms and corresponding BJH pore-size distribution curves (inset) for as-synthesized samples. (b) UV-Vis absorption spectra of the prepared samples. (c) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of N,S-GQDs. (d) the curves of (ahν)1/2 versus hν for prepared microrods.

(a) Adsorption of Cr (VI) under dark; (b) photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI); (c) the photocatalytic stability of 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 sample; (d,e) the photoelectric current curves and EIS Nyquist plots of the prepared samples, respectively; (f) photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the fabricated samples.
The photocatalytic mechanism for Cr6+ reduction over N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 composite microrods under light irradiation.
BET surface area, total pore volume and average pore size of Bi2S3 and N,S-GQDs doped Bi2S3 microrods.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of N-S-GQDs/Bi2S3 microrods with highly photocatalytic performance for Cr(VI) reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Key Laboratory of Estuarine Ecological Security and Environmental Health, Xiamen University Tan Kah Kee College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Key Laboratory of Estuarine Ecological Security and Environmental Health, Xiamen University Tan Kah Kee College, Zhangzhou 363105, Fujian, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Develop new method to deal with heavy metal pollution is one of important topics in environmental protection. In this work, we prepared novel N,S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) modified Bi2S3 microrods (N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3) via a facile one-step hydrothermal method. In hydrothermal process, the produced N,S-GQDs were synchronously doped into Bi2S3 crystals. The introduction of N,S-GQDs largely increases the specific surface area of the catalyst and accelerates the migration of photogenerated carriers. Moreover, N,S-GQDs doping significantly enhanced the light absorption of Bi2S3 crystals in the range of 200–800 nm. The photocatalytic performance of the prepared catalyst was evaluated by photocatalytic reduction removal of aqueous Cr(VI). The results showed that the performance of the composite N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 microrods was significantly improved compared with bare Bi2S3. With optimum molar ratio of N,S-GQDs to Bi2S3, the reduction rate for Cr(VI) to Cr(III) over N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 reaches 70.3% in 80 min. This work provides a simple route for constructing graphene quantum do-s doped metal sulfide semiconductor as a highly efficient photocatalyst for chromium reduction under light irradiation.

N,S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) modified Bi2S3 microrods displayed high efficiency for aqueous Cr(VI) removal.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions, e.g., Cr (VI), Hg(II) and Pb(II), in wastewater are hazardous pollutants which could directly or indirectly damage the ecological environment or harm human health. The photocatalytic technology is an attractive and promising method to deal with environmental problems. It can use sunlight to remove heavy metal ions and other organic pollutions in wastewater [1–5]. Bi2S3 is an important visible-light-driven material. Compared with traditional TiO2, g-C3N4, perovskite and other photocatalysts, Bi2S3 has some distinct advantages, e. g., strong visible light absorption, easy synthesis and low cost. Moreover, the distinct photocatalytic property in reduction of heavy metal ions and hydrogen production over Bi2S3 crystals could be obtained [6,7].

The photocatalytic performance of Bi2S3 crystals is strongly dependent on their microstructure, morphology, composition and fabrication method. To get high photocatalytic performance, various methods have been developed to modify the Bi2S3, such as element doping [8–10], heterojunction construction [11–13], morphology regulation [14–16] and etc. For example, Wu et al. [17] prepared uniform colloidal Bi2S3 nanodots and nanorods with different sizes by a hot injection method. The obtained Bi2S3 nanocrystals performed high efficiency in photodegradation of Rhodamine B. Qiao et al. [18] prepared n-n Bi2MoO6/Bi2S3 heterojunctions, which displayed high performance in photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI). Another Bi2S3/Bi2O3/Bi2O2CO3 composite photocatalyst was prepared by combination the heat-treatment process with ion exchange technique. The produced ternary photocatalyst possessed strong ability to degrade different organic pollutants (formaldehyde gas, methyl orange and phenol) [19]. Moreover, this ternary catalyst can remove more than 99% of formaldehyde gas within 100 min, which is significantly higher than that of pure Bi2O2CO3(10.9%) and Bi2O3/Bi2O2CO3(23.6%) samples under the same conditions. However, the preparation process for this ternary catalyst is relatively complicated.

The unique physicochemical properties of carbon‐based material have attracted great interest for improving the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts [20,21]. graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a typical of carbon‐based materials with optimizing the band gap, enhancing visible light capture, accelerating charge transfer, and improving photogenerated carrier separation, enabling them to significantly boost photocatalytic performance. Therefore, after the loading of GQDs, the improved photocatalytic performance was observed, mainly including photocatalytic degradation of dyes [22,23], photocatalytic removal of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) [24], photocatalytic nitrogen fixation [25], photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [26,27], and photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) [28]. It is also reported that N and S doping can narrow the band gap and extend the visible light absorption region of GQDs [23,27].

As for the fabrication of GQDs doped semiconductors, the traditional method is that GQDs are firstly synthesized. Then GQDs were combined with other semiconductor through stirring or hydrothermal treatment. The GQDs doped catalysts prepared by traditional method are always confronted with low stability, time-consuming and unsatisfactory performance. Here, we developed a simple and efficient method to prepare N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 composite microrods. The obtained N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 microrods performed excellent photocatalytic performance in Cr(VI) reduction, which could provide valuable information for facile fabrication of GQD doped metal sulfide photocatalytic materials with promising application.

All chemicals used in this experiment are analytical grade. Thiourea (CH4N2S), trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7.2H2O), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3.5H2O), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), 1,5-Diphenylcarbazide (C13H14N4O), acetone (CH3COCH3) and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. China.

Typically, the N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 was firstly synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. 0.97 g Bi(NO3)3.5H2O and 0.2284 g thiourea were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water at room temperature, respectively. Then, different stoichiometric of sodium citrate (0.0588 g, 0.1176 g and 0.2352 g) was added into the above solution, respectively. After stirring vigorously by magnetic for 1 h, the prepared solution was transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor, which was heated to 200 °C for 24 h. After the solution was cooled down to room temperature, the gray sample was washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol alternately by centrifugation for 3 times. Finally, the powder was placed in a drying oven at 60 °C to obtain x-N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 sample. Here, x indicates the molar ratio of sodium citrate to Bi2S3.

To compare the influence of fabrication method on photocatalytic, another N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 sample was synthesized by a traditional two-step method. 0.97 g Bi(NO3)3.5H2O and 0.2284 g thiourea were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water at room temperature. After stirring vigorously for 1 h, the solution was placed in a hydrothermal reactor, which was heated at 200 °C for 24 h to obtain Bi2S3. 0.228 g thiourea and 0.294 g citric acid were mixed and placed in a hydrothermal reaction kettle, which was reacted for 24 h at 200 °C. After the sample was naturally cooled down to room temperature, the N,S-GQDs was obtained. Finally, the N,S-GQDs and Bi2S3 were mixed and stirred at room temperature for 24 h, the produced sample was washed alternately with deionized water and absolute ethanol three times to obtain N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 (traditional)[29].

Powder XRD-ray diffractometer (Rigaku’s Ultima IV) was used to analyze the purity and crystallinity of the produced sample (Cu Ka, λ=0.15418 nm, working voltage:40 KV, current: 40 mA, scanning rate: 5°/min). The BET test was carried out on an automatic analyzer (ASAP2020) at 77 K. The morphology of the sample was analyzed using the Regulus 8220 Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 20) was used to analyze the microstructure of catalyst. The UV–vis DRS test (Japan Shimadzu, UV-2550) used BaSO4 as the reference with the scanning range was 200–800 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis was tested by KBr tablet method on an infrared spectrometer (Nicolet-6700). The Raman test was performed on a Thermo Fisher's DXR2 Raman spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) was obtained over a Hitachi instrument (F-4500) and the excitation wavelength is 450 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer. The photoelectric measurements were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E A15459) with a standard three-electrode configuration (the obtained sample as the working electrolyte, Ag/AgCl as reference electrode, platinum wire as counter electrode), 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 aqueous solution as the electrolyte.

A potassium dichromate solution with a mass concentration of 10 mg/L was prepared to produce aqueous Cr(VI), and the light source was a 350 W metal halide lamp (Wavelength range: 380–800 nm). 15 mg catalyst and 50 mL Cr(VI) solution were poured into a quartz test tube. The electromagnetic stirrer was turned on and after stirring for 180 min in the dark the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was obtained. Then, the metal halide lamp was turned on, and the photocatalytic activity measurement was performed, and samples were taken every 30 min. The diphenylcarbazide spectrophotometric method was used to measure Cr(VI) concentration [30]. The photocatalytic reduction efficiency is described as C/C0. Here, C0 represents the initial concentration of Cr6+ (mg/L), and C represents the concentration of Cr6+(mg/L) at each sampling time point.

The crystal phase and crystallinities of the obtained samples were investigated by powder X-ray diffraction patterns. As shown in Fig. 1, we clearly observe that the labeled diffraction peaks of the Bi2S3 correspond to orthorhombic Bi2S3 crystal (JCPDS no.84-0279) [31]. No diffraction peaks attributed to other impurities was found in Fig. 1. With doped N,S-GQDs, a new characteristic peak can be observed at 25.86°, which corresponded well to the (002) crystal plane of graphite[27]. Notably, the (002) peak slightly shifts to high angles as the N,S-GQDs concentration increases, reflecting the contraction of the interlayer. In addition, no other obvious diffraction peaks were detected for the N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 samples, indicating their good purity [32].

Surface chemical bonds in the obtained samples were first demonstrated by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2a, the FT-IR spectra of N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 are similar to that of pure Bi2S3, revealing that the basic structures of Bi2S3 were preserved. Moreover, the appeared C-C bonds at 1025 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra indicated that N,S-GQDs were successfully doped into Bi2S3 [33] . In addition, the wide absorption band at 3000–3500 cm−1 can be attributed to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, which shows that the GQDs surface contains a large number of amino groups and hydroxyl groups [34].

Raman spectroscopy is a very sensitive test technology to analyze the local chemical interactions of the nanomaterials. From the Raman spectra of pure Bi2S3 (black line) and 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 (pink line) shown in Fig. 2b, some similar characteristic peaks for prepared sample can be found. The peaks at 1392 cm−1 and 1569 cm−1 are attributed to the D and G bands of doped materials, respectively [27,35–37]. The peak at 1392 cm−1 originated from the existence of sp3 defects, while the peak at 1569 cm−1 is related to the plane vibration of sp2 carbon [34]. This result is consistent with the previous report.

The surface chemical compositions of the typical 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 microrods were further investigated by XPS. In Fig. S1, the XPS spectra of the fabricated pure N,S-GQDs shows the elements of C, N and S, indicating the N and S were doped into GQDs. In Fig. 2c, the XPS survey spectrum depicts that the Bi, S, C and N elements were detected over the 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 sample. As shown in Fig. 2d, the peaks at 158.69 eV and 158.30 eV can be attributed to the signal of Bi-S bonds, while the peaks with binding energies of 164.03 eV and 163.67 eV are from the Bi-O bond [31]. The XPS spectrum for C 2s in 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 (Fig. 2e) shows that the peaks at 284.25 eV and 287.65 eV are corresponding to C-C bond and CO bond, respectively [38–40]. The S2s spectrum clearly shows the characteristic peak of S2- at 224.63 eV [41,42]. In addition, the N 1s could be identified two different peaks, where the peaks at 532.5 eV and 530.8 eV are assigned to the (C)3-N(sp3) and N-H(sp3), respectively (Fig. 2f), further confirming that the N,S-GQDs were successfully doped into Bi2S3 [43,44].

To analyze the structural and morphological information of the prepared samples, SEM and TEM analysis were performed. Fig. 3a demonstrates that the pure Bi2S3 appears spherical structure composed of the rod-like crystals. In Fig. 3b and c, compared to bare Bi2S3, the morphology of Bi2S3 doped with 0.8N,S-GQDs and 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 becomes a dispersed rod shape particles. The structure of this dispersed microrods could beneficial to the adsorption of substrates in water, which in turn promotes the photocatalytic reaction. Fig. 3d depicts the TEM image with low-magnification for the as-obtained 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 and the careful observation reveals the uniformly distributed N,S-GQDs with the diameter of 2–8 nm on the surface of Bi2S3. As shown in Fig. 3e,f, the clear lattice boundaries in the HRTEM images illustrate the high crystallinity of the 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 nanocrystals. Meanwhile, the observed lattice fringes over the two samples are determined to be 0.301 nm and 0.355 nm, which corresponds to the (211) and (101) crystal plane of Bi2S3 [31,45], respectively. In addition, a lattice spacing of 0.278 nm, which corresponding to the (1120) lattice fringes of nitrogen doped graphene, can be easily found in obtained-sample, indicating that the graphene quantum dots were successfully doped into Bi2S3 crystals [46,47].

To study the specific surface area of the obtained samples, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore distribution curves were tested. As shown in Fig. 4a, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of 0.8N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 and 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 are all of representative type-IV curve with a H3-type hysteresis loops, which indicated the presence of mesoporous structure. The pore size distribution curves (inset of Fig. 4(a)) of all the samples were estimated from 2 to 65 nm by using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method, showing the characteristic of mesoporous and microporous structures. In addition, the BET surface area, total pore volume and average pore size for the obtained samples were summarized in Table 1. The BET surface area of 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 was calculated to 25.30 m2/g, which is about 100 times of that of pure Bi2S3 (0.24 m2/g). Generally speaking, a large specific surface area can provide more active sites for adsorption and reaction, which helps to improve photocatalytic activity [22].

The light absorption properties of the samples were studied by solid UV-Vis DRS. As shown in Fig. 4b, all the catalysts exhibit strong absorption in entire ultraviolet-visible spectral region. Compared with bare Bi2S3, the light absorption intensity of the as-prepared samples with doped N,S-GQDs are significantly enhanced in the range of 200–800 nm. Meanwhile, the light absorption edges of the synthesized N,S-GQDs are around 231 nm and 310 nm (Fig. 4c), respectively [43,48,49], which shows that using sodium citrate and thiourea as raw materials N,S-GQDs can be prepared by hydrothermal method [27]. The plots of (ahv)2 versus hv of the samples are shown in Fig. 4d. According to the (ahv)2= hv-Eg [50], we can conclude that the optical band gap energies (E
g ) of Bi2S3, 0.8N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 and 1.0N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 are 0.85 eV, 1.11 eV and 1.88 eV, respectively.

Fig. 5a shows the adsorption rate for Cr(VI) over the prepared samples under dark. The 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 sample exhibits better performance for Cr(VI) adsorption than that of N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 (traditional) and Bi2S3. At 180 min, the Cr(VI) over catalysts can reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After reaching adsorption equilibrium, the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) was implemented under metal halide lamp irradiation. Fig. 5b shows the reduction rate of Cr(VI) over different samples. The N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 samples exhibit better performance for reduction of Cr(VI) than that of Bi2S3. Particularly, 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 exhibits the highest activity for Cr(VI) reduction. However, the photocatalytic performance of 1.2N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 is lower than that of 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3, which can be attributed to high nitrogen and sulfur content will result in more oxygen vacancies, and the oxygen vacancies fill the impurity state, thereby inhibiting the photocatalytic activity [51]. After 80 min of irradiation, the reduction rate of Cr(VI) over 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 sample reached 70%, which was about 4 times that of pure Bi2S3 sample (17.3%), and better than the N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 (traditional) (51.6%). It is worth noting that high adsorption performance of the catalyst for Cr(VI) is conducive to photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) in water [52]. In addition, the supported N,S-GQDs on bismuth sulfide can enhance the light absorption, which helps to improve the photocatalytic activity. When the doped amount of N,S-GQDs is too high, the agglomeration of N,S-GQDs could lead to the decrease of active sites and the reduction of photocatalytic activity.

To further study the photocatalytic stability of 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 sample, the recycling test was performed. From Fig. 5c, we can see that 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 exhibits better photocatalytic stability than N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 (traditional), which shows that for the fabrication of N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3, the one step hydrothermal method is better than traditional two-step method.

To reveal the reason for the difference in catalytic performance, the electrochemical measurement and PL spectroscopy were used to evaluate the photoelectric conversion efficiency, carrier migration and combination properties of photocatalysts. Fig. 5d shows that the photocurrent intensity of the samples with N,S-GQDs are much stronger than that of bare Bi2S3. The highest photocurrent in 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 reflects the best light response and highest efficient carrier separation, resulting the best photocatalytic activity. EIS spectrum is usually used to analyze the carrier transfer process. Fig. 5e displays the AC impedance diagram and it clear shows that the radius of the arc of the N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 sample is smaller than that of pure Bi2S3, and 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 shows the minimum arc radius, indicating that the presence of N,S-GQDs can reduce the migration resistance of photogenerated electrons [53]. Moreover, the small charge transfer resistance could beneficial to charge migration and separation. PL spectrum is an effective mean to reveal the separation and recombination efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers, which indirectly reflects the recombination of electron–hole pairs [54]. The lower emission intensity of PL implies the better separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. From Fig. S2, we can see that the prepared N-GQDs exhibit a emission peak at 442 nm and N,S-GQDs exhibit a small peak at 451 nm. These two peaks are similar to the PL emission peak for GQDs (460 nm) [33]. As displayed in Fig. 5f, the as-prepared composite samples exhibit the PL peak similar to that of Bi2S3 with a strong and broad emission peak centered at 670 nm. The PL intensity of 1.0N,S-GQDs-Bi2S3 is the weakest, indicating the recombination rate of photoinduced electrons and holes is obviously suppressed. In summary, loading N,S-GQDs can accelerate the separation rate of photogenerated electrons and holes on the catalyst.

The positions of energy bands of catalyst can be figured out according to the formula (1) and (2):
(1) ECB=X–0·5Eg+E0
(2) EVB=ECB+Eg

here ECB and ECB represent the conduction band edge and valence band edge, and Eg is the band gap energy. X is the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor and E0 is a measurement scale factor of the redox level of the reference electrode relative to the absolute vacuum scale (−4.5 eV vs NHE) [55]. The X for N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 can be figured out to be 5.28 eV. Based on the band gap of N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3(1.11 eV) according to UV–vis (Fig. 4), we can conclude that the potentials for ECB and EVB of N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 are 0.23 eV and 1.34 eV, respectively.

As shown in Scheme 1, N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 can be excited under the irradiation of metal halide lamp. All wavelengths of light can be absorbed by Bi2S3, and then electrons are excited to an excited state. At the same time, N,S-GQDs can absorb visible light[56], and then excites short-wavelength light (λ<470 nm) to be absorbed by Bi2S3, which further promotes Bi2S3 to generate more photo-generated carriers[46,49]. Since the N atom has a strong electron affinity in N,S-GQDs, the electrons excited over Bi2S3 are easily transferred to N,S-GQDs, which accelerates the separation of electron-hole pairs. Therefore, the coupling of N,S-GQDs effectively promotes the photocatalytic reduction reaction. This mechanism accounts for why N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 displayed better photocatalytic activity in Cr6+ reduction than pure Bi2S3.

N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 composite microrods were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The introduction of N,S-GQDs changes the morphology of Bi2S3 and greatly increases the specific surface area and improves the adsorption of Cr(VI). Moreover, the doped N,S-GQDs can accelerate the separation of photogenerated electron and hole pairs. Therefore, the improved catalytic activity for Cr(VI) reduction over N,S-GQDs/Bi2S3 was obtained.

Zhenmin Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing. Xingqiang Liu: Writing - original draft. Wanqin Zhou: Visualization. Changlin Yu: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration. Fang Li: Validation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Guangdong Province Universities and Colleges Pearl River Scholar Funded Scheme (2019), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2019A1515011249, 2021A1515010305), Key Research Project of Natural Science of Guangdong Provincial Department of Education (2019KZDXM010), Science and Technology Project of Maoming (2020KJZX035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127109.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The enhanced photoreduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) using carbon dots coupled TiO2 mesocrystals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.X. Zhang, M.J. Xu, H. Li, H. Ge, Z.F. Bian, The enhanced photoreduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) using carbon dots coupled TiO2 mesocrystals, Appl. Catal. B Environ. 226 (2018) 213‐219.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel SiO2 nanoparticle-decorated BiOCl nanosheets exhibiting high photocatalytic performances for the removal of organic pollutants
